coredata.dc:title: Battery condition monitoring using spectral centroid of active acoustic emission signals
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Lei
Li, Zhen
Miao, Haiyan
Zeng, Wenhan
Gu, Fengshou
Ball, Andrew D.
coredata.dc:description: Condition monitoring of State of Charge (SOC) and State of Health (SOH) is critical for ensuring the safety, reliability, and performance of lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) across a wide range of applications. Acoustic sensing has emerged as a promising technique for characterizing internal battery states in a non-invasive manner. This study introduces a novel acoustic methodology for real-time monitoring of SOC in pouch-type LiBs during charge–discharge cycles, as well as SOH following calendar aging, using spectral centroid (SC) analysis of frequency-swept active acoustic signals. Key contribution includes the development of a hybrid modeling framework integrating finite element (FE) simulations with experimental validation. SOH was evaluated by repeating on the same batteries after 5-month storage under controlled laboratory conditions. The results provide clear evidence of mechano-electrochemical coupling effects resulting from lithium-ion intercalation/deintercalation, solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth, and loss of active material (LAM) during both cycling and aging processes. The correlations observed between acoustic features and SOH offer a possible cost-effective and scalable enhancement to existing non-destructive evaluation (NDE) techniques. This research establishes an acoustic-guided wave-based diagnostic framework capable of tracking dynamic changes in material properties, thereby facilitating early failure mode detection and supporting the development of predictive maintenance strategies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries (LiBs)
SOC and SOH monitoring
Acoustic emission (AE) signal sensing
Non-destructive evaluation (NDE)
Spectral centroid (SC)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Setup for FE simulation and experiment.

Finite element analysis on acoustic wave propagation on PLA and LiB at SOC100. (a) PLA and (b) LiB, excitation signal at 1.5 MHz (c) PLA and (d) LiB, excitation signal at 1 MHz, (e) PLA and (f) LiB, excitation signal at 500 kHz.
Analysis of WPD on (a) time domain and (b) frequency domain comparison, Root mean square (RMS) comparison in time domain (c) and frequency domain (d) of raw and processed signals.
Normalized AE spectrum with (a) 3D samples and pouch cell at SOC100, (b) pouch cell1 when at SOC0, SOC50 and SOC100.
Resonance frequency peaks SC shift of when Cell 1 was in (a)(b) charging and (c)(d) discharging process.
Voltage vs Current vs Capacity drop of the four datasets.
(a) Normalized AE response comparison on Cell 1 at different aging status (b) SC Comparison of at different frequency bands.
Summary of calculation when waves are propagating in the LiBs.

head.ce:title: Battery condition monitoring using spectral centroid of active acoustic emission signals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Efficiency and Performance Engineering, University of Huddersfield, Huddersfield, HD1 3DH, UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Efficiency and Performance Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Centre for Efficiency and Performance Engineering, University of Huddersfield, Huddersfield, HD1 3DH, UK.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Efficiency and Performance Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Condition monitoring of State of Charge (SOC) and State of Health (SOH) is critical for ensuring the safety, reliability, and performance of lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) across a wide range of applications. Acoustic sensing has emerged as a promising technique for characterizing internal battery states in a non-invasive manner. This study introduces a novel acoustic methodology for real-time monitoring of SOC in pouch-type LiBs during charge–discharge cycles, as well as SOH following calendar aging, using spectral centroid (SC) analysis of frequency-swept active acoustic signals. Key contribution includes the development of a hybrid modeling framework integrating finite element (FE) simulations with experimental validation. SOH was evaluated by repeating on the same batteries after 5-month storage under controlled laboratory conditions. The results provide clear evidence of mechano-electrochemical coupling effects resulting from lithium-ion intercalation/deintercalation, solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth, and loss of active material (LAM) during both cycling and aging processes. The correlations observed between acoustic features and SOH offer a possible cost-effective and scalable enhancement to existing non-destructive evaluation (NDE) techniques. This research establishes an acoustic-guided wave-based diagnostic framework capable of tracking dynamic changes in material properties, thereby facilitating early failure mode detection and supporting the development of predictive maintenance strategies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LiBs), prized for their high energy density and versatility, constitute indispensable power sources across diverse sectors including electric vehicles, energy storage systems, consumer electronics, military equipment, and aerospace engineering [1]. Ensuring their safe operation and long-term reliability through sophisticated battery management systems (BMS) has thus become imperative. Contemporary BMS architectures critically depend on precise monitoring of key operational parameters - particularly State of Charge (SOC) and State of Health (SOH) - alongside real-time tracking of voltage, current, and temperature [2]. Precise SOC estimation is fundamental for optimizing performance, preventing hazardous conditions (e.g., overcharging), and maximizing lifespan, employing methods ranging from conventional techniques to sophisticated algorithms like Kalman filters and neural networks. Recent reviews comprehensively categorize health prognostic methods into short-term SOC estimation, long-term end-of-life (EoL) prediction, and degradation trajectory forecasting, highlighting the interplay between aging mechanisms (e.g., loss of lithium inventory (LLI) and loss of active material (LAM)) and external factors like temperature and load profiles [3,4]. Besides, advancements in data-driven approaches, exemplified by self-supervised Informer networks that integrate multimodal data alignment to leverage temporal dependencies and partial charging data for high-accuracy SOC prediction, further highlight the potential of intelligent monitoring strategies [5].

However, LiBs face significant durability challenges arising from coupled electrochemical-mechanical degradation mechanisms during cyclic operation [6,7]. The intercalation/deintercalation of lithium ions induces dynamic volumetric changes in electrode materials, generating internal stress fields through concentration gradient-driven diffusion processes [8]. Furthermore, degradation mechanisms involve repetitive electrode expansion/contraction, progressive shifts in mechanical properties (such as Young's modulus), and microstructural alterations including porosity changes, collectively accelerating capacity fade and increasing failure risks [9]. Critically, the dynamic evolution of LiB's internal mechanical state during operation is intrinsically linked to its electrochemical status.

This interdependence necessitates robust non-destructive evaluation (NDE) techniques for operational state monitoring of batteries. While numerous methods exist for analyzing state parameters, including in-situ/in-operando optical imaging, X-ray diffraction, neutron tomography, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) [10,11], ultrasonic sensing has gained prominence due to its high sensitivity to material property changes, non-invasive operation, rapid scanning capability for real-time monitoring, and multi-mode propagation features that suit complex geometries [12,13]. Guided wave technology leverages multi-mode characteristics to offer flexibility in selecting sensitive modes and excitation methods for intricate structures, with propagation dynamics inherently reflecting both structural integrity and mechanical properties of the medium [14]. Such approaches align with multimodal frameworks that align time-series signals with textual knowledge for fault diagnosis, enhancing interpretability through cross-modal learning [15]. Building on this, acoustic emission (AE) techniques have emerged as complementary NDE tools, with active AE methods presenting distinct advantages. In general, non-destructive methods can be categorized into active and passive setups. Active AE involves externally generating waves (e.g., ultrasound) to probe material internals, whereas passive AE detects naturally occurring signals from internal events like cracking [16].

For instance, active acoustic analysis employs pulse-echo or through-transmission techniques to assess wave propagation changes, enabling precise detection of microstructural variations. In contrast, traditional ultrasonic sensing focuses on wave velocity or attenuation shifts, while passive AE identifies transient events like particle fracture or gas evolution without external excitation. Active AE excels in providing controlled, reproducible signals for early degradation diagnosis such as microcracking or delamination in LiBs, outperforming passive AE in signal-to-noise ratio and specificity for pre-determined testing scenarios. Its ability to integrate with electrochemical metrics further allows for correlating mechanical changes with performance loss, a challenge for methods relying solely on historical data [17,18].

Previous studies have laid the groundwork for acoustic applications in batteries. Pioneered work by Pecht et al. [19], demonstrated its efficacy in identifying internal electrode wrinkles and swelling via transmission signals. Subsequent studies correlated acoustic parameters (time-of-flight (ToF), amplitude) and SOC in reflection/transmission configurations, while machine learning approaches enabled functional SOC/SOH prediction. Theoretical models, Biot theory formulations and guided wave propagation frameworks [20–23], further advanced correlations between acoustic features and electrochemical states. However, these are often emphasized ultrasonic modalities; active AE extends this by capturing real-time, mechanics-driven events, such as solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) formation or silicon particle cracking, through tailored waveform analysis. Notably, active AE's innovation lies in its dual capacity for targeted interrogation of battery components (e.g., via frequency-specific triggers) and synergy with other techniques like EIS, offering a multi-parametric health assessment that pure ultrasonic methods may lack [24,25], This positions active AE as a versatile tool for non-invasive battery monitoring, particularly in dynamic applications like electric vehicles, where early fault detection is in much higher priority. Despite these advances, existing non-invasive techniques often fail to simultaneously capture dynamic electrochemical-mechanical evolutions during operational cycling and aging. While acoustic sensing shows promise for internal state characterization, a comprehensive framework integrating real-time SOC tracking during charge–discharge, SOH assessment after extended storage, and mechanistic understanding of underlying coupling phenomena is underexplored. To address this, this work develops a robust, cost-effective diagnostic tool bridging simulation-driven insights with experimental validation. We establish a novel approach that spectral centroid (SC) analysis of frequency-swept active waves phenomena. By systematically evaluating SOC/SOH evolution through repeated measurements on aged cells, we uncover acoustic signatures correlated with degradation modes, advancing NDE capabilities toward scalable predictive maintenance. Ultimately, this study provides a physics-informed acoustic-guided wave framework for early failure detection, supporting the safe, reliable deployment of LiBs across diverse applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of LiBs SOC and SOH evaluation by acoustic emission signals
ce:section.ce:para: Acoustics-based detection techniques have emerged as essential tools for assessing the internal condition of LiBs, offering a non-invasive approach to ensure operational safety and performance monitoring.

Serving as a critical diagnostic tool for LiBs, AE enables the precise detection and characterization of internal structural alterations such as gas evolution, electrode deformation, and crack propagation [26]. The frequency-dependent attenuation characteristics of acoustic wave propagation through battery components demonstrate strong correlations with evolving electrochemical states. These changes, induced by variations in SOC and SOH, manifest through microstructural modifications including electrode particle cracking, SEI evolution, and lithium plating [27].

While acoustic attenuation generally correlates with battery electrochemical states, conventional single-frequency measurements face limitations in characterizing multi-parameter degradation phenomena. Broadband excitation through frequency-swept acoustic signals addresses this challenge by enabling simultaneous multi-frequency interrogation within defined temporal windows. This approach captures comprehensive spectral information essential for state assessment through energy attenuation mapping of transmitted signals [28]. The resulting time-frequency spectrograms identify frequency-dependent attenuation profiles, establishing correlations with SOC, SOH and degradation modes. This methodology provides a novel framework for non-invasive battery diagnostics while maintaining compatibility with operational environments [29].

The complex aging mechanisms of LiBs can be abstracted into fundamental degradation modes, primarily LLI and LAM at the anode or cathode [30,31]. Charging/discharging relies on lithium intercalation into (and deintercalation from) active material. Therefore, total capacity depends inherently on the amount of active material and mobile lithium. Core aging thus reduces the capacity of LAM and LLI [32,33].

Additional critical degradation modes are increased internal resistance (IRI) and electrolyte loss (LE). IRI directly leads to power fade and can also reduce useable capacity under constant voltage limits. While minor LE may have negligible initial impact, significant electrolyte loss, including depletion of critical additives, can precipitate rapid capacity decline at end-of-life.

These macroscopic modes stem from complex, coupled internal physicochemical side reactions. For instance, LAM arises from graphite exfoliation, metal dissolution, or loss of electrical contact (due to current collector corrosion, binder failure, or LE). LLI mainly comes from SEI/Cathode Electrolyte Interphase (CEI) growth and lithium plating. LE itself results from consumption during SEI/CEI formation, high-voltage decomposition, and evaporation. IRI is affected by SEI growth and LE [31,33,34]. Reaction rates depend on battery design, manufacturing quality, and operating conditions, all of which ultimately determine the battery's lifespan and require detailed discussion.

Regarding primary degradation modes, calendar aging and cycle aging are governed by disparate driving forces and mechanistic pathways. While calendar aging is predominantly driven by thermodynamic instabilities, where persistent chemical potential differences at elevated temperatures and SOC trigger gradual side reactions, such as the continuous growth of the SEI and electrolyte oxidation. These reactions lead to a uniform and time-dependent loss of LLI and a slow increase in internal resistance. In contrast, cycle aging is primarily induced by electrochemical and mechanical stresses during charge-discharge operations. Key mechanisms include mechanical fatigue and particle cracking of active materials due to repetitive volume changes, lithium plating under high-current or low-temperature charging, and phase transformation-induced structural damage. These processes result in a more pronounced and cumulative LAM and accelerated LLI [35–37].

The decrease in porosity primarily stem from several interconnected aging mechanisms. First, the continuous growth and thickening of the SEI layer clog electrode pores, leading to an approximate 5% reduction in porosity from initial values during normal cycling [38]. For example, lithium intercalation swells graphite, further lowering porosity. Second, lithium plating (especially at low temperatures/fast charging) forms dendritic or mossy lithium, blocking pores and speeding up porosity loss [13]. Additionally, gas generation from electrolyte decomposition (e.g., CO2, CH4) may temporarily increase local pore space due to bubble formation, but long-term gas accumulation promotes electrolyte dry-out and interface failure, ultimately reducing overall porosity [39]. Mechanical degradation, such as particle cracking and binder failure, also contributes to porosity reduction, although microcrack formation may briefly increase localized porosity. Overall, porosity declines monotonically but nonlinearly: an initial rapid decrease phase dominated by SEI growth, a stable mid-phase, and an accelerated late stage driven by lithium plating and gas accumulation [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wave propagation theories in the LiBs
ce:section.ce:para: In the acoustic testing, bulk waves refer to ultrasonic waves within a material. Theoretical frameworks governing their propagation in the LiBs are classified in Table 1 . During electrochemical cycling, material property changes can significantly influence mechanical characteristics, impacting key measurement parameters such as ToF and signal amplitude (SA).

To predict V
p , typical battery models such as composite homogenization model (CHM), Biot's model is taken into consideration. CHM treats the electrode model as an isotropic medium, wave velocities are given by equations (1)–(3) in Table 1, where linear changes during cycling affect V
p and then ToF [21]. Furthermore, Knehr et al. [37] introduced the term effective modulus to encapsulate the elastic interactions within a cell's multilayered structure into a single, homogenized parameter. This approach models the cell as an isotropic medium, justified by the condition that the characteristic acoustic wavelength significantly exceeds the thickness of individual constituent layers. Within this framework, shear modulus contributions are deliberately neglected to simplify the model. The effective modulus formulation inherently accounts for emergent effects on acoustic wave velocity arising explicitly from the layered architecture.

Additionally, approximate evaluation limits the validity of CHM, therefore, Biot's Model is introduced to illustrate how waves propagate in fluid-saturated porous media. According to Biot's theory, when experimental frequency is way below the transition frequency determined by equation (4) [40], the fast and slow velocity and the one from shear waves V
p , V
L , V
s can be obtained by equations (5)–(8) respectively [41,42].

As another critical acoustic parameter, SA varies during cycling primarily from changes in coefficients of acoustic attenuation and transmission at material interfaces. Attenuation analysis is complex due to battery material anisotropy, porosity, and viscoelasticity [42,43]. SA at distance x from the source in a homogeneous material is defined by equation (9) in Table 1, indicating that changes in material damping during cycling are believed to alter α
i , consequently affecting SA [44,45].

What's more, SA magnitude is governed by acoustic impedance mismatches at interfaces between different battery layers (e.g., current collector/electrode, electrode/separator). Acoustic signals undergo reflection and transmission governed by equation 10–12 [46].

Based on discussion above, to evaluate the macroscopic trends of SOC and SOH, kHz-level acoustic signals are employed to probe potential material alterations in LiBs. Specifically, lithium-ion intercalation and porosity evolution induce measurable volume changes within the battery structure; meanwhile, the formation of large gas bubbles and progressive electrolyte depletion can also be detected through acoustic signatures [13,47].

As illustrated in Fig. 1 , the detection process by utilizing frequency-swept AE signals for LiBs is divided into several sections: finite element (FE) simulation and validation, frequency-swept signal construction by AE signal generator, experiment by comparing different samples under test, wavelet packet decomposition (WPD) denoising, data processing and analysis.

To validate the suitability of kHz-level acoustic signals for this study, preliminary FE simulations detailed in following chapter were performed. Specifically, two configurations were tested: a polylactic acid (PLA) sample and a simplified pouch cell sample, both subjected to acoustic excitation at 500 kHz, 1 MHz, and 1.5 MHz respectively.

According to Knehr et al. [37], a pouch cell can be treated as an isotropic medium when the generated acoustic wavelength significantly exceeds the thickness of its individual layers. Defining the effective detection bandwidth was therefore a critical methodological step. For this purpose, FE analysis was employed to determine the optimal sweep frequency range. As illustrated in Fig. 2 , a simplified pouch cell with identical volume to the real test cell was positioned between an ultrasonic transducer and an AE sensor. The transducer and sensor locations indicated in Fig. 2 were optimized to ensure a sufficiently long acoustic propagation path within the simplified LiB, facilitating clearer observation of transmission properties.

Further structural context is provided by Tancogne-Dejean et al. [48], who characterized the jelly-roll-like internal morphology of undeformed LiBs via micrographs (detailed architectures are shown in their published figures). The undeformed jelly roll comprises alternating layers of anodes (copper foil coated with graphite), cathodes (aluminum foil coated with a lithium-based metal oxide), and porous polymeric separators, where a liquid electrolyte occupies ∼40% of the cell volume and forms a continuous pore network. This porous architecture evident from distinct layer interfaces and void spaces dominates the non-monotonic stress-strain response under mechanical loading. To bridge structural understanding to acoustic signal propagation, a PLA sample was fabricated with geometry identical to the real pouch cell, leveraging its analogous porous features to effectively simulate acoustic wave propagation through simplified material. Thus, FE analysis was conducted to verify the feasibility of mimicking LiB's porous behavior using such PLA proxies.

For the experimental setup, Analog Discovery 3 Oscilloscope (configured as the waveform generator), produced high-fidelity linear frequency-swept signals with programmable parameters. Key settings with sweep duration (10 s), frequency range (100-500 kHz) and excitation waveform (sine wave) which were precisely programmed via computer interface prior to transducer activation.

The configured sweep signal was amplified through a power amplifier with 40 dB gain maintaining consistent amplification throughout testing. This amplified signal subsequently drove a piezoelectric transducer (100-500 kHz, 1 MHz center frequency) acoustically coupled to the pouch cell, generating controlled broadband acoustic excitation.

Finally, WPD was applied to address inherent challenges in propagating AE signals within LiBs. As a multi-resolution analysis tool, WPD overcomes limitations of conventional methods by decomposing signals into finer frequency sub-bands, enabling extraction of subtle features corresponding to potential material changes in LiBs across varying SOC and SOH. This approach is pivotal for online quality control, as it enhances sensitivity to parameter-dependent variations, including LLI and lithium plating-induced LAM, which directly modulate acoustic responses during battery degradation.

To demonstrate the previous theoretical assumptions and verify the reliability and superiority of the proposed feature extraction algorithm, this chapter outlines FE simulation conducted by COMSOL Multiphysics and experiments designed to collect AE signals under various working conditions.

The parameters applied in the simulation are described in Table 2 . As illustrated in Fig. 3 , for excitation frequencies exceeding 1 MHz, longitudinal waves are observable in simulations, enabling the penetration process to be approximated as a one-dimensional elastic response. However, as the frequency decreases to 500 kHz, the wavelength increases significantly approaching both the thickness of individual layers and the overall thickness of the tested pouch cell (see Table 3 ). This scaling allows wave propagation through the entire battery to be observed, where interactions with porous microstructures become prominent and correlate strongly with the battery's collective mechanical-electrochemical behavior; concurrently, increased attenuation arises from scattering and dissipation at electrode interfaces at these lower frequencies.

Initial transmission experiments validated these frequency-dependent trends: frequencies swept from 100 to 500 kHz across three 3D-printed PLA samples, and one fully charged pouch cell revealed characteristics governed by microstructural features. Furthermore, subsequent comparative experiments from Fig. 5 demonstrate that time-frequency analysis uncovers another inherent response signature from the LiB. This capability can further isolate the battery's intrinsic dynamic response range during degradation, ensuring that observed spectral features originate from the LiB itself rather than artifacts of propagation or coupling.

The experiment apparatus is facilitated as indicated in Fig. 2. Prior to the experiment, a couple of normalization tests were conducted. Three 3D-printed samples with PLA were installed between the AE sensor (specifications can be found in Table 5
) and the piezoelectric transducer. Acoustic couplant were used between the samples, AE sensor and transducer, in order to make sure proper contact between the two surfaces. Then, two RS Pro 3.7V pouch cells with a multi-layered structure were located in the same position where the samples had been. Both cells were connected to the battery testing system to conduct a complete cycle test. Specifications of the 3D printed samples, and the pouch cell are listed below.

The ambient temperature was constant at room temperature 25 °C. The cells were first charged with 0.25C-rate (0.5 A) until the cut-off current reached 0.1A, the constant voltage at 4.2 V. After 30 min rest, the cells were then discharged (cut-off voltage at 3 V) with constant current (0.25C, 0.5 A). Meanwhile, the AE test was conducted on the sample per 10-min interval.

In this work, WPD was employed to decompose the raw AE signals as mentioned, sampled at 5 MHz into Wavelet Packet Components (WPCs), which were sampled at 5 MHz. A five-level decomposition using the Daubechies 3 (db3) wavelet was performed, yielding 32 WPCs with a uniform frequency bandwidth of approximately 78.125 kHz per component. To focus on the frequency range of interest (100–500 kHz), WPCs 2 through 6, which collectively span from approximately 78 kHz to 469 kHz, were selected and reconstructed to form the feature signal. This approach enables precise time–frequency analysis of the AE signals within the target band. The distribution and overlap of the selected WPCs are illustrated in Fig. 4
.

Key parameters of the tested cells are summarized in Table 4. To further analyze the SC, we normalized the frequency responses of the experimental system, 3D-printed samples, and pouch cells full of charge—results are presented in Fig. 5(a). It can be observed that both PLA samples and the pouch cell exhibit overlapping distinct frequency response features in the 250–300 kHz range, whereas unique acoustic frequency signatures of the pouch cell are excited by acoustic signals in the 300–400 kHz range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results of LiBs at different SOCs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, resonance frequency responses of a single pouch cell across different SOC states were normalized and presented in Fig. 5(b). A distinct frequency shift is observable in the 250–300 kHz range, whereas the shift in the 350–400 kHz range is far less pronounced.
Furthermore, resonance frequency shift can be clearly observed as shown in Fig. 6 in both charge and discharge processes.

To probe deeper into these trends, results from charging Cell 1 clearly show that resonance frequency peaks exhibit a slow upward drift during charging—reversing direction during discharge. Fariñas et al. [49], who applied wideband pulse excitation to pouch cells, attributed this behavior to the cell's thickness changes, framing it within the broader “breathing effect.”

Despite the coexistence of both bulk and guided waves in our experiments, investigation and analysis focused primarily on bulk wave characteristics by correlating mechanical property variations with SOC. Consistent with prior work, as SOC increases, lithium-ion intercalation into the graphite anode drives lattice expansion and thickens the electrode, while cathode delithiation may induce structural contraction. Critically, the elastic modulus of electrode materials depends strongly on lithium content, which accelerates acoustic signal propagation within the battery [50]. Conversely, Cell 1 exhibits the opposite trend during discharge.

Furthermore, during the first charge/discharge cycle, electrolyte reduction on the graphite anode surface forms a SEI membrane composed primarily of Li2CO3, LiF, and other organic/inorganic species, which inhibits further electrolyte decomposition. Over subsequent cycling, the SEI breaks down and regenerates owing to mechanical stress (from electrode expansion/contraction) and side reactions (e.g., high-temperature electrolyte degradation), consuming both electrolyte and active lithium. Meanwhile, electrolyte decomposition generates gases (e.g., CO2, CH4) via oxidation pathways, while lithium plating produces H2 through parallel chemical reactions [51–54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results of SOH on the same LiB after calendar aging loss
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, four datasets from the same pouch cell configuration are analyzed: Cell 1 and Cell 1# correspond to the initial and final states of the first test group, while Cell 1 aged and Cell 1 aged# represent the initial and final states of the same group after 5 months of storage. The capacity loss can be observed from Fig. 7 during the period.

Resonance frequency responses of the normalized signals from these four datasets are presented in Fig. 8 (a). Overlapped features are observed in the 200–250 kHz, 250–350 kHz, 350–400 kHz, and 400–500 kHz ranges—likely arising from unchanged material properties of the pouch cell.

Examining Fig. 8(b), in the 200–250 kHz range, all four responses are nearly identical but exhibit distinct separation between SOC 60 and SOC 100: Cell 1 and Cell 1 # display higher responses than Cell 1 aged and Cell 1 aged #. Interestingly, in the 250–350 kHz range, the aged group shows higher frequency responses, though their energy distributions (Fig. 8(a)) differ. The trends in the 350–400 kHz and 400–500 kHz ranges align with the full-band features and are consistent with capacity loss driven by LLI, LAM and other material degradation mechanisms discussed earlier. The normalized amplitude of responses in the 100–200 kHz range is too low to warrant further discussion.

As discussed, The LLI mechanism is predominantly attributed to SEI formation and repair cycles, where solvent reduction deposits insulating compounds, depleting lithium inventory over time. For instance, the growth of the SEI layer follows a diffusion-limited process, modeled by equations accounting for temperature-dependent activation energies. Concurrently, LAM occurs due to volumetric changes during lithium intercalation/deintercalation, causing fracture in active particles or delamination from current collectors.

These processes are consistent with findings that calendar aging is influenced by factors such as temperature and SOC, with higher values intensifying degradation rates. The integration of multi-mechanism models, which couple SEI growth, electrolyte dry-out, and mechanical stress, provides a comprehensive framework for predicting these shifts, as validated through degradation mode analysis [5,53,54].

The study investigates time-frequency characteristics by focusing on resonance peak shifts in the spectral centroid during charging/discharging and calendar aging with multiple approaches. Key findings are summarized as follows.
1.
FE simulations demonstrate that, based on the sensitivity frequency of the selected AE transducer and isotropic model, kHz-level acoustic signals enable precise characterization of LiBs by detecting variations of the pouch cell in porous features, volume changes, and capacity loss stemming from LLI and lithium plating-induced LAM.
2.
Comparative experiments between 3D-printed PLA samples and pouch cells identify common acoustic features arising from porous characteristics shared by both materials in the same shape. FE models guide acoustic signal interpretation by simulating wave propagation in both 3D-printed porous PLA and cells, while experiments validate analysis. This hybrid approach bridges simulation-driven insights, providing mechanistic understanding of degradation and SOC/SOH state evaluation. Notably, due to the LiB's complex structure, AE signals detect unique acoustic signatures beyond these overlapping features.
3.
Resonance peak shifts are also clearly observable in the SC during both charging and discharging. Peaks shift toward higher frequencies as SOC increases and revert as SOC decreases. These shifts are attributable to both thickness changes and other mechanical variations (e.g., Young's modulus, Poisson's ratio, density), reflecting coupled physical and electrochemical dynamics during operation.
4.
During calendar aging, additional resonance frequency shifts occur due to degradation mechanisms such as LLI and LAM, which drive irreversible capacity fade. These shifts correlate with underlying physicochemical alterations, including electrode densification, porosity reduction, and modified mechanical properties (e.g., effective modulus, interfacial stiffness).

This work establishes an acoustic-guided wave framework for advanced LiB diagnostics, enabling ND tracking of dynamic material property changes and supporting the development of predictive maintenance protocols via early failure mode identification. The experimentally validated correlations between acoustic signatures and key electrochemical states constitute a potential cost-effective, practical enhancement to NDE techniques, addressing critical needs for real-time battery health monitoring. Furthermore, this methodology delivers feasible insights for optimizing LiB design, and improving operational management by linking measurable acoustic features to the underlying physicochemical aging mechanisms elucidated in prior studies.

Lei Fu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhen Li: Methodology. Haiyan Miao: Supervision. Wenhan Zeng: Supervision. Fengshou Gu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Andrew D. Ball: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
